title: Adding Data (INSERT INTO)
description: Welcome a new user to your app.

slides.title: More than just reading 📖
slides.content:
# We need new data!

Up until now, we've only been reading existing data using `SELECT`.

But when a user signs up for your app, or buys a product, the app has to write *brand new* data into the database!

slides.title: The INSERT Command 📤
slides.content:
## Adding a new row

To insert data, we use two keywords: **`INSERT INTO`**, followed by the table name.

Then, we list the columns we want to fill out inside parentheses.

`INSERT INTO users (first_name, email)`

quizData.question: What is the very first part of an insertion statement telling the database?

options.text: What data actually goes into the rows
Which table we are putting data into, and exactly which columns we intend to provide data for
It tells the database to close

quizData.explanation: You must physically declare which exact table and columns you are targeting before providing the actual values.

slides.title: Providing the Values 📥
slides.content:
## Handing over the data

After specifying the table and columns, we use the **`VALUES`** keyword to provide the actual data inside another set of parentheses.

`INSERT INTO users (first_name, email) VALUES ('John', 'john@gmail.com');`

The order must perfectly match! 'John' goes to first_name, and 'john@gmail.com' goes to email.

quizData.question: If you write `INSERT INTO users (age, name) VALUES ('Alice', 25);`, what will happen?

options.text: The database crashes because it tries to put 'Alice' into the age column
It automatically figures out Alice is a name and sorts it properly

quizData.explanation: Databases are very dumb. They blindly map the first value you provide to the first column you declared. The order must be absolutely identical.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Add a new row to the `movies` table. Pass the `title` and `year` columns, with the values `'Inception'` and `2010`.
